%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.3-1 Sketching Exponential Signals

> **Section Objective:** Learn to sketch monotonic exponential signals quickly and accurately for exams — without plotting dozens of points.

This section is about three things you must own before moving on: how the **sign in the exponent** controls whether a signal decays or grows, how to **read the time constant** directly from the formula, and how to **place anchor values** that let you draw a clean sketch in seconds.

This matters because exponentially varying sinusoids — covered next — are built on exactly this foundation. Every sketch you do later will reuse the same logic.

#### Critical Warning

Do not confuse the time constant with the parameter \(a\). The safe rule: for \(e^{-at}\) with \(a > 0\), the time constant is \(\tau = 1/a\), not \(a\).

## 1. Decay vs Growth from the Exponent

For \(a > 0\), the signal \(e^{-at}\) **decreases monotonically** as \(t\) increases, while \(e^{at}\) **increases monotonically**. Monotonic simply means the signal moves in one direction only — no oscillation, no turning back.

Before you do anything else with an exponential signal, look at the **sign attached to \(a t\) in the exponent**. That sign is the first decision:

- Negative sign \(\Rightarrow\) decay
- Positive sign \(\Rightarrow\) growth

### EXAM DISTINCTION

The parameter \(a\) sets the **speed** of change. The sign sets the **direction** of change. These are two separate pieces of information.

A larger positive \(a\) in \(e^{-at}\) means faster decay, because the time constant \(\tau = 1/a\) becomes smaller.

$$e^{-at},\quad e^{at},\quad \tau = \frac{1}{a}\;(a>0)$$
*The minus sign in the exponent produces decay, the plus sign produces growth, and for the decaying form \(e^{-at}\) the time constant is \(\tau = 1/a\).*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.10](/figures/page-021-fig__b_10-1.png)
*After one time constant \(\tau = 1/a\), the exponential falls to \(1/e\) of its current value — not by a fixed amount, but by a fixed multiplicative factor.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Equal time-constant intervals produce equal multiplicative drops by \(1/e\) — the spacing in time is uniform, but the spacing in amplitude is not.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215399372-8034.png)

## 2. How to Sketch Using the Time Constant

Here is a fast exam procedure. Use \(x(t) = e^{-2t}\) as the working example.

**Step 1:** Identify \(a = 2\).

**Step 2:** Compute the time constant: \(\tau = 1/a = 1/2 = 0.5\).

**Step 3:** Mark the starting value: \(x(0) = 1\).

**Step 4:** Place anchor points — \(x(0.5) = 1/e \approx 0.37\) and \(x(1) = (1/e)^2 \approx 0.135\).

**Step 5:** Draw a smooth, monotonically decreasing curve through those three anchors.

Each additional interval of length \(\tau\) multiplies the current value by \(1/e\) again. You never need to compute from scratch.

### HIGH-SCORE INSIGHT

If two time instants are separated by exactly one time constant, the ratio of the signal values is always \(1/e\) — regardless of where on the curve you are. This ratio rule is more robust than memorizing isolated points, because it works at any starting position on the curve.

$$\frac{e^{-at_2}}{e^{-at_1}} = e^{-a(t_2-t_1)} = \frac{1}{e}\quad \text{when } t_2-t_1=\frac{1}{a}$$
*Any two points on the curve that are exactly one time constant apart always have the same value ratio \(1/e\), which is why the anchor-based sketching rule works at any location on the curve, not just from the origin.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Negative exponent sign means decay; positive means growth — check the sign before anything else.
- TRAP: the time constant is \(\tau = 1/a\), not \(a\) — confusing these is the most common exam error.
- Each time-constant interval multiplies the value by \(1/e\); the drop is multiplicative, not subtractive.

*In the next section we will use the same sketching mindset for exponentially varying sinusoids.*
